topics.headline: California's $128B TK-12 budget locks in a 4.31% COLA and a $5B one-time block grant — charter schools included
topics.why_matters: The $128 billion portion of the 2026-27 budget grew by about $400 million from what Newsom proposed in May, and school districts and charter schools will receive a larger-than-projected cost-of-living increase of 4.31%, which includes funding to cover up to 14 weeks of paid maternity leave for all school staff. Critically for A+'s HOT 13 and Tier A partners, the Legislature's budget includes $1 billion more for community schools, a $2.4 billion boost to ongoing special education funding and a $5 billion one-time block grant that districts and charter schools can spend however they want. That $5B is discretionary money A+ partners can braid with Title III for LTEL intervention right now.
topics.angle: "The biggest state education budget in California history just passed. Here's what the $5B discretionary block grant means for your intervention plan before the school year starts."
topics.roman_take: Record-high funding removes the last excuse for LTEL stagnation — the money is on the table; the constraint is execution capacity, not dollars.
topics.danielle_take: Walk through how to sequence the $5B one-time block grant against existing Title III subgrants so schools don't leave 2024-25 carryover on the table when 2026-27 starts. ---

topics.headline: iLEAD Flex opens in Lancaster August 2026 — first mover as the NCB moratorium ends
topics.category: C (Tier A partner news) with A (state policy) overlay
topics.why_matters: iLEAD announced expansion into Lancaster with a new charter school in their network beginning in 2026, with iLEAD Flex opening in August 2026. The California moratorium on new nonclassroom-based charters concluded on January 1, 2026, and iLEAD — already a Tier A A+ partner across Antelope Valley, Lancaster, Exploration, Hybrid, and Online — is one of the first networks moving. The TK–12 campus offers families a choice between full classroom-based instruction, Independent Study, or hybrid options on campus each week. New site + new enrollment pipeline = new intervention needs on day one.
topics.angle: Congratulate iLEAD on the Lancaster launch and frame the moratorium's end as the beginning of a new capacity-planning era for flex-based schools — where intervention infrastructure is the differentiator, not marketing.
topics.roman_take: The schools who win the next five years won't be the ones who opened first — they'll be the ones who built the reclassification and Tier 2/3 systems before the state made them.
topics.danielle_take: When a charter opens a new site, the intervention plan can't be an afterthought — Title III and Title I services need to be scoped into the LCAP before the doors open, not audited into existence later.

topics.headline: LAUSD's court-mandated tutoring rollout is running a live vendor bake-off — and every district in California is watching
topics.why_matters: This is the largest live competitive comparison in the tutoring category anywhere in the country. LAUSD is now officially on the hook, following a court-approved settlement, to provide high-dosage tutoring to more than 100,000 TK-12 students — more than a quarter of the district. In a statement, LAUSD said it is conducting a program evaluation that will explore variation in the implementation, take-up, and impact on student outcomes across a range of tutoring models and vendors. More than a quarter of the district's TK-12 students will receive a mix of virtual and in-person sessions, delivered in small groups or one-on-one to complement classroom instruction. Every charter director in California will be reading the eventual LAUSD evaluation report. A+ can front-run that conversation by defining what "fidelity" actually looks like, using its own reclassification data, before Varsity Tutors and the AI-tutor vendors write their own version of the narrative.
topics.angle: "The biggest tutoring RFP in California isn't an RFP — it's a court settlement. Here's what LAUSD's vendor evaluation should be measuring."
topics.roman_take: When a mandate forces a district to buy tutoring at scale, procurement almost always wins the fight against pedagogy — and that's why most district-scale tutoring underdelivers. The vendors who "win" LAUSD will be the ones with the lowest bid, not the ones with the highest reclassification rate.
topics.danielle_take: A "mix of virtual and in-person sessions" across 100,000 students is only as good as the intervention tracking behind it. Roll-up dashboards don't move CAASPP scores; small-group fidelity with a documented dosage floor does — and that's the operational bar a charter can hold a vendor to before signing. ---
